Context & Memory Management.description: How should an LLM agent decide what to keep in its limited context window? We're interested in approaches that dynamically select, retrieve, or compress information at inference time — this includes per-query context synthesis, conversation history retrieval with relevance gating, automatic summarization and compaction of past turns, hierarchical task decomposition, and event-driven memory that preserves milestone moments while filtering noise. The common thread is agent-level context management, not model-internal mechanisms like KV cache or attention heads.

Agent Memory Architecture & State Tracki.description: How should an agent organize its memory across different timescales and abstraction levels? We're looking for work that proposes or evaluates structured memory architectures — for instance, separating fast-changing working state, episodic traces of what happened, and slower-changing semantic or procedural knowledge. Also in scope: agents that maintain explicit internal world models, track environment state independently of trajectory logs, handle belief states or POMDPs, and manage dependencies between pieces of state. The key distinction from context management is that we're interested in the architecture of memory — how information is layered and organized — not just how to select or compress it.

Retrieval Heads & Selective Attention in.description: Microscopic attention head analysis applied to agent-level context management. Retrieval heads that specialize in finding key information across long sequences. Non-uniform KV cache management — selective retention based on head function. NOT about model training — about inference-time head-aware context selection.

Formal Verification.description: AI/LLM-driven approaches to formal verification, automated theorem proving (especially with neural/LLM methods), and LLM-assisted program/code verification.

Autonomous Agent & Tool Evolution.description: LLM agents that autonomously discover, extract, compose, create, and evolve their own skills/tools. Skill extraction from code/demos, tool learning and tool composition for LLMs, agent self-improvement, automatic tool creation, skill-based architectures, and self-directed automation workflows.

Cognitive Science.description: Cognitive science and neuroscience insights that inform AI/LLM design — working memory mechanisms, metacognition, self-monitoring, human-like reasoning patterns applied to neural architectures.

Agent Code Verification, Harness Self-Ev.description: How do LLM agents cheat, and how can formal verification or better evaluation design prevent it? We're interested in: reward hacking during RL training (agents that exploit grader/test flaws — hardcoding answers, deleting failing tests, patching verify()), specification gaming in code benchmarks, code agent exploits of verifiers and evaluation frameworks, benchmark contamination and leakage, and verification debt in autonomous coding systems. Also in scope: self-evolving agent frameworks with verification gating, harness-as-code evolution, and mitigation strategies against reward hacking (robust optimization, reward shaping). Out of scope: general AI safety not specific to code agents, or security exploitation of production systems (pentesting, vulnerability scanning) that doesn't involve agent-level reward gaming.

Agent Goal Representation & Inner Alignm.description: What goal does an LLM actually internalize when given an optimization task — the semantic target we describe, or a proxy metric it can manipulate? We're interested in the empirical and mechanistic study of goal formation in LLMs: goal misgeneralization (the model learns a different goal than intended, even from a correct specification), mesa-optimization (RLHF creates internal objectives that diverge from both pretraining and reward), alignment faking (models appearing aligned during training but pursuing hidden goals), activation steering and representation engineering (probing and manipulating internal goal representations), and situational awareness in LLM agents. We focus on work with empirical evidence — behavioral experiments, probing studies, causal interventions — not pure alignment philosophy. Out of scope: general AI safety without mechanistic grounding, or value alignment theory without testable predictions.

Formal Methods for Kernel & Operator Opt.description: We're interested in a specific methodology for operator / kernel optimization: a formal specification defines correct behavior, then automated search finds the most efficient low-level implementation, and formal verification (equivalence checking, theorem proving) guarantees correctness. In scope: superoptimization of any target (GPU kernels, tensor programs, assembly, bytecode, circuits, RTL), equivalence checking used for optimization, verified compilation and kernel synthesis, LLM-driven kernel/code generation with formal correctness guarantees, formal verification of floating-point or numerical code, and any approach that combines formal methods with performance optimization at the operator or low level. The target domain doesn't matter — it can be GPU kernels, WebAssembly, quantum circuits, or RTL — as long as the workflow involves formal correctness arguments plus optimization. Out of scope: general formal verification of existing code without an optimization component, algorithm-level optimization without operator/kernel concerns, or ML-based optimization with no formal correctness argument.
